draft.introduction: Maximus the Confessor’s Mystagogia (c. 630 CE) is a liturgical commentary interpreting the church building and the Eucharistic liturgy as icons of the entire cosmic and spiritual order. It synthesizes Dionysian symbolism with Chalcedonian Christology.

translation.text: The holy church of God is an image and type of the entire cosmos. For the cosmos consists of visible and invisible natures, and so does the church consist of nave and sanctuary. The church is also an image of the human person, who is a microcosm. And conversely, the human person is a church in miniature.
translation.original_text: τὴν ἁγίαν ἐκκλησίαν τοῦ θεοῦ τύπον καὶ εἰκόνα τοῦ σύμπαντος κόσμου

translator_notes.note: Maximus’s triple analogy—church as cosmos, church as human person, human person as church—creates a network of symbolic correspondences that makes the liturgy a participation in cosmic reality. This is theosis enacted ritually: the worshiper becomes what the church symbolizes.
